edges.target: TSHR-IGF1R Receptor Crosstalk in Orbital Fibroblasts

evidence.snippet: Clinical features include thyroid enlargement, eye signs, tachycardia, heat intolerance, emotional lability, weight loss, and hyperkinesis.

evidence.reference_title: Graves' disease. Manifestations and therapeutic options.
evidence.snippet: Graves' disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism. Clinical features include thyroid enlargement, eye signs, tachycardia, heat intolerance, emotional lability, weight loss, and hyperkinesis.

evidence.reference_title: Fracture Incidence in Graves' Disease: A Population-Based Study.
evidence.snippet: Graves' disease (GD) is an autoimmune disease, and it is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism.
Steatorrhea is due to hyperphagia and stimulation of the adrenergic system.

nodes.description: In thyroid eye disease, cooperative signaling between thyrotropin receptor (TSHR) and insulin-like growth factor 1 receptor (IGF-1R) on orbital fibroblasts drives hyaluronan production, adipogenesis, and tissue remodeling causing proptosis.
nodes.id: TSHR-IGF1R Receptor Crosstalk in Orbital Fibroblasts

nodes.description: FDA-approved anti-IGF-1R monoclonal antibody for moderate-to-severe active thyroid eye disease. Blocks TSHR-IGF1R crosstalk in orbital fibroblasts to reduce proptosis, diplopia, and clinical activity. Validated by the OPTIC Phase III trial.

evidence.snippet: Among patients with active thyroid eye disease, teprotumumab resulted in better outcomes with respect to proptosis, Clinical Activity Score, diplopia, and quality of life than placebo; serious adverse events were uncommon.

nodes.description: Excessive stimulation of the thyroid gland results in increased production of thyroid hormones (thyroxine, T4, and triiodothyronine, T3).

evidence.reference_title: Thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor and its role in Graves' disease.
evidence.snippet: In Graves' disease, thyroid-stimulating autoantibodies can mimic TSH action and stimulate thyroid cells. This leads to hyperthyroidism and abnormal overproduction of thyroid hormone.

Common causes are Graves' disease, toxic multinodular goitre and toxic solitary nodule. Excess thyroid hormones in the circulation are also found in thyroiditis (hormone leakage) and excess exogenous thyroxine intake.

evidence.reference_title: Increased production of B-cell growth factor by T lymphocytes in Graves' thyroid: possible role of CD4+ CD29+ cells.
evidence.snippet: BCGF production by T cells from the thyroid or peripheral blood of Graves' patients, in the absence or presence of PHA, was significantly greater than that by peripheral T cells from healthy controls.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Graves disease: a global overview.
evidence.snippet: Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder characterized by goitre, hyperthyroidism and, in 25% of patients, Graves ophthalmopathy. The hyperthyroidism is caused by thyroid hypertrophy and stimulation of function, resulting from interaction of anti-TSH-receptor antibodies (TRAb) with the TSH receptor on thyroid follicular cells.
Clinical features include thyroid enlargement, eye signs, tachycardia, heat intolerance, emotional lability, weight loss, and hyperkinesis.
